questions.stem: What does TF-IDF measure in the context of text representation?

options.A: The total frequency of a word in a document collection
options.B: A word's importance to a document relative to a corpus — high if frequent in the document but rare across the corpus
options.C: The semantic similarity between two documents
options.D: The number of unique tokens in a vocabulary

questions.explanation: TF-IDF = TF(t,d) × IDF(t). TF: how often term t appears in document d. IDF = log(N/df(t)): penalises terms common across all N documents. High TF-IDF indicates a term is distinctive to that document. Used in search engines and classical text classification.
questions.source: Manning et al. Introduction to Information Retrieval. Cambridge UP, 2008. Ch.6. https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book

questions.stem: Which tokenisation approach splits text into subword units to handle out-of-vocabulary words, and is used by BERT and GPT?

options.A: Whitespace tokenisation
options.B: Character-level tokenisation
options.C: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) or WordPiece subword tokenisation
options.D: Sentence-level tokenisation

questions.explanation: BPE (Sennrich et al., 2016) iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs into subword tokens. WordPiece (used in BERT) is similar. Both handle OOV words by decomposing them into known subword units (e.g., 'unhappiness' → 'un', '##happi', '##ness'). GPT uses BPE; BERT uses WordPiece.
questions.source: Sennrich et al. Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units. ACL, 2016. https://arxiv.org/abs/1508.07909

questions.stem: Why do dense word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) outperform one-hot encoding for most NLP tasks?

options.A: One-hot vectors are larger, making them slower to compute
options.B: Dense embeddings encode semantic similarity in a continuous vector space — synonyms have similar vectors, enabling generalisation; one-hot vectors are orthogonal and encode no semantic relationships
options.C: One-hot encoding cannot represent words with more than 26 letters
options.D: Dense embeddings reduce vocabulary size

questions.explanation: One-hot: V-dimensional sparse binary vector — every word is equidistant. Word2Vec/GloVe: d-dimensional dense vectors (d=100–300) trained so similar words have high cosine similarity (e.g., king−man+woman≈queen). This semantic geometry enables generalisation across related words — a critical advantage for downstream tasks.
questions.source: Mikolov et al. Distributed Representations of Words. NeurIPS, 2013. https://arxiv.org/abs/1301.3781; Pennington et al. GloVe. EMNLP, 2014.

questions.stem: How do static word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) differ from contextualised embeddings (BERT, ELMo) in handling polysemy?

options.A: Static and contextualised embeddings are identical — both produce one vector per word
options.B: Static embeddings assign a single fixed vector per word regardless of context; contextualised embeddings produce different vectors for the same word depending on its surrounding tokens, correctly distinguishing senses (e.g., 'bank' in financial vs river contexts)
options.C: Contextualised embeddings are only available for English
options.D: Static embeddings are more accurate for polysemous words

questions.explanation: Word2Vec/GloVe: one vector per word type — 'bank' (financial) and 'bank' (river) share one embedding. ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT produce token-level contextualised embeddings — the representation of 'bank' changes based on surrounding tokens. Critical for disambiguation and downstream task performance.
questions.source: Peters et al. Deep Contextualised Word Representations (ELMo). NAACL, 2018. https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.05365

questions.stem: You have 50,000 product reviews for sentiment classification. Which text representation pipeline is most appropriate for fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model?

options.A: TF-IDF vectorisation → logistic regression (no BERT fine-tuning)
options.B: BERT WordPiece tokenisation → truncate/pad to 512 tokens → add [CLS] token → fine-tune encoder with classification head using AdamW optimiser
options.C: Word2Vec embeddings → average pooling → SVM classifier
options.D: Character-level one-hot encoding → LSTM → softmax

questions.explanation: BERT fine-tuning pipeline: (1) WordPiece tokeniser with [CLS] and [SEP] tokens; (2) max 512 token limit with padding/truncation; (3) extract [CLS] embedding from final encoder layer; (4) add linear classification head; (5) fine-tune all layers with AdamW (weight decay decoupled) at lr=2e-5 for 3–5 epochs. Standard achieves ~94% on SST-2.
questions.source: Devlin et al. BERT. NAACL, 2019. https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805

questions.stem: What is Named Entity Recognition (NER) in NLP?

options.A: Classifying the overall sentiment of a document as positive or negative
options.B: Identifying and classifying named entities (persons, organisations, locations, dates) in text
options.C: Translating text from one language to another
options.D: Generating a shorter version of a document

questions.explanation: NER is a sequence labelling task: for each token, assign an entity class (PER, ORG, LOC, DATE, MISC) or O (outside). Modern NER uses transformer-based models (BERT + token classification head) achieving F1>90% on CoNLL-2003. Applications: information extraction, knowledge graph construction, search.
questions.source: Manning et al. Introduction to Information Retrieval. 2008; Devlin et al. BERT. 2019.

questions.stem: Which metric is standard for evaluating machine translation quality, and what does it measure?

options.A: ROUGE — measures recall of n-gram overlap between output and reference
options.B: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) — measures precision of n-gram overlap between the machine translation output and one or more human reference translations
options.C: Accuracy — measures the proportion of exactly correct translations
options.D: Perplexity — measures the probability the model assigns to the test set

questions.explanation: BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002): computes modified n-gram precision (n=1–4) between hypothesis and references, with a brevity penalty to discourage short outputs. BLEU∈[0,1]; higher=better. Limitations: does not capture meaning or fluency. ROUGE is used for summarisation (recall-oriented).
questions.source: Papineni et al. BLEU: a Method for Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation. ACL, 2002. https://aclanthology.org/P02-1040

questions.stem: Why is the sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) architecture with attention preferred over a basic encoder-decoder for long-sequence machine translation?

options.A: Seq2seq with attention uses fewer parameters
options.B: Basic encoder-decoder compresses the entire source sentence into a single fixed-length context vector — an information bottleneck for long sequences. Attention allows the decoder to dynamically focus on relevant source positions at each decoding step
options.C: Attention mechanisms eliminate the need for an encoder
options.D: Basic encoder-decoder is preferred — attention only helps for short sequences

questions.explanation: Bahdanau et al. (2015): the fixed context vector bottleneck causes performance degradation for sentences >30 tokens. Attention computes a context vector cₜ = Σᵢ αₜᵢhᵢ at each decoder step, where αₜᵢ = softmax(score(sₜ₋₁, hᵢ)) — selectively attending to relevant encoder states. This directly motivated the Transformer's self-attention.
questions.source: Bahdanau et al. Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate. ICLR, 2015. https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473

questions.stem: How does extractive summarisation differ from abstractive summarisation, and what are the trade-offs?

options.A: Extractive uses neural networks; abstractive uses rule-based methods
options.B: Extractive selects and concatenates sentences verbatim from the source — factually faithful but less fluent; abstractive generates new text using seq2seq/transformers — more fluent and concise but prone to hallucination
options.C: Abstractive is always more accurate than extractive
options.D: Both approaches produce identical output quality

questions.explanation: Extractive: sentences scored by importance (TextRank, BERT-based) and top-k selected verbatim — no hallucination risk, but may be repetitive or miss cross-sentence synthesis. Abstractive (BART, T5, PEGASUS): generates novel summary sentences — more coherent but may introduce factual errors (hallucination). Critical distinction for high-stakes applications (legal, medical).
questions.source: Lewis et al. BART. ACL, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.13461; Zhang et al. PEGASUS. ICML, 2020.

questions.stem: A BERT-based NER model achieves 92% overall accuracy on a test set but only 61% F1 on the DISEASE entity class (5% of tokens). Diagnose and propose two remediations.

options.A: 92% accuracy is excellent — no improvement needed
options.B: Class imbalance: DISEASE entities are rare (5%), so the model under-predicts them. High overall accuracy masks poor minority-class F1. Remediations: (1) oversample DISEASE-tagged sentences or apply class-weighted loss; (2) augment training data with domain-specific medical corpora (e.g., fine-tune on i2b2/NCBI Disease corpus)
options.C: Reduce the model size to improve minority-class performance
options.D: Switch from F1 to accuracy as the evaluation metric

questions.explanation: DISEASE = 5% of tokens → severe imbalance. Model defaults to predicting O (outside) for ambiguous tokens. Fix 1: class-weighted cross-entropy — upweight DISEASE class loss. Fix 2: domain adaptation — pre-train or fine-tune on biomedical text (BioBERT, PubMedBERT) which has seen medical entity patterns. Both are validated in the clinical NLP literature.
questions.source: Lee et al. BioBERT. Bioinformatics, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.08746; Uzuner et al. i2b2 NLP Challenge. JAMIA, 2011.

questions.stem: What is 'hallucination' in the context of large language models?

options.A: The model generating text that is grammatically incorrect
options.B: The model confidently generating factually incorrect or fabricated information not supported by its training data or the provided context
options.C: The model refusing to answer a question
options.D: The model producing outputs that are too short

questions.explanation: LLM hallucination: the model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect statements — fabricated citations, wrong dates, invented statistics. Caused by the model optimising for fluency/coherence rather than factual accuracy. Key safety concern for high-stakes applications (medical, legal, financial).
questions.source: Ji et al. Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation. ACM CSUR, 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.03629

questions.stem: What does RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) achieve in LLM training?

options.A: It increases the model's parameter count
options.B: It aligns LLM outputs with human preferences by training a reward model on human comparisons, then fine-tuning the LLM using PPO to maximise the reward — reducing harmful, toxic, or unhelpful outputs
options.C: It reduces the computational cost of pre-training
options.D: It enables the model to browse the internet in real time

questions.explanation: RLHF pipeline (InstructGPT, Ouyang et al., 2022): (1) supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations; (2) train reward model on human preference rankings; (3) RL fine-tuning (PPO) to maximise reward. Result: models that follow instructions, refuse harmful requests, and produce more helpful responses. Used in GPT-4, Claude, Gemini.
questions.source: Ouyang et al. Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT). NeurIPS, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155

questions.stem: Why does increasing context window length (e.g., from 4K to 128K tokens) significantly increase the computational cost of Transformer inference?

options.A: Longer contexts require more training epochs
options.B: Self-attention complexity is O(n²) in sequence length n — quadrupling context length increases attention computation 16×, and KV-cache memory grows linearly with context length
options.C: Longer contexts require larger embedding dimensions
options.D: Context length has no effect on computational cost

questions.explanation: Self-attention: for each of n tokens, compute attention over all n tokens → O(n²) time and memory. At n=4K: 16M operations. At n=128K: 16B operations — 1,000× increase. KV-cache: stores n key-value pairs per layer per request — memory grows linearly. Solutions: FlashAttention, sliding window attention (Longformer), sparse attention.
questions.source: Vaswani et al. NeurIPS, 2017; Dao et al. FlashAttention. NeurIPS, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135

questions.stem: How does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) reduce LLM hallucination compared to purely parametric generation?

options.A: RAG reduces hallucination by increasing the LLM's parameter count
options.B: RAG retrieves relevant documents from an external knowledge base at inference time and conditions the LLM's generation on those documents — grounding responses in verifiable source text rather than relying solely on memorised training data
options.C: RAG eliminates the need for pre-training
options.D: RAG reduces hallucination by filtering outputs through a grammar checker

questions.explanation: RAG (Lewis et al., 2020): query → dense retrieval (DPR/vector DB) → top-k documents → LLM generates conditioned on retrieved context. Benefits: (1) grounds responses in source documents; (2) knowledge can be updated without retraining; (3) sources can be cited. Hallucination risk remains if retrieved documents are irrelevant or the model ignores them.
questions.source: Lewis et al. Retrieval-Augmented Generation. NeurIPS, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401

questions.stem: You are building a customer support chatbot using an LLM. The bot must answer questions about your company's products using only the official product documentation. Which architecture best prevents hallucination?

options.A: Fine-tune GPT-4 on product documentation and deploy without retrieval
options.B: Implement RAG: embed product documentation in a vector database, retrieve top-3 relevant chunks per query, prompt the LLM with retrieved context and an explicit instruction to answer only from provided documents, and return source citations
options.C: Use a rule-based chatbot with no LLM
options.D: Use an LLM with no grounding — its training data likely includes product information

questions.explanation: Fine-tuning (A) bakes knowledge into weights but still hallucinates and cannot be updated without retraining. RAG (B): retrieval grounds each response in current documentation; system prompt instruction ('answer only from context') further constrains generation; citations allow users to verify. Best practice for enterprise knowledge-base applications. Update docs → update vector DB, no retraining needed.
questions.source: Lewis et al. RAG. NeurIPS, 2020; Gao et al. Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP. 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.10997

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · Prompt Engineering and LLM Evaluation

questions.stem: What is few-shot prompting in the context of LLMs?

options.A: Training the LLM on a small dataset of labelled examples
options.B: Including a small number of input-output examples in the prompt to demonstrate the desired task format and behaviour, without updating model weights
options.C: Reducing the model's output length to a few sentences
options.D: Using a smaller, distilled version of the LLM

questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting (Brown et al., GPT-3, 2020): provide k examples of (input, output) pairs in the context window before the query. The model infers the task pattern from examples without gradient updates — 'in-context learning'. Contrasts with zero-shot (no examples) and fine-tuning (weight updates).
questions.source: Brown et al. Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3). NeurIPS, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165

questions.stem: Which prompting technique asks the LLM to explicitly reason step-by-step before producing a final answer, improving performance on multi-step reasoning tasks?

options.A: Zero-shot prompting
options.B: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting
options.C: Temperature scaling
options.D: Top-p sampling

questions.explanation: Chain-of-Thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022): add 'Let's think step by step' or provide reasoning examples. The model externalises intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer. Significantly improves accuracy on arithmetic, logical, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks (e.g., +20pp on GSM8K).
questions.source: Wei et al. Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models. NeurIPS, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903

questions.stem: What does the temperature parameter control in LLM text generation, and what are the effects of setting it to 0 vs 1?

options.A: Temperature controls the model's training speed — higher values train faster
options.B: Temperature scales the logits before softmax, controlling output randomness. Temperature=0: greedy decoding — always selects the highest-probability token (deterministic, repetitive). Temperature=1: samples proportionally to model probabilities (creative, diverse). Temperature>1: increasingly random.
options.C: Temperature controls the context window length
options.D: Temperature has no effect on output diversity

questions.explanation: Softmax with temperature: P(token) = exp(logit/T) / Σexp(logitsⱼ/T). T→0: near-one-hot distribution → greedy selection. T=1: standard softmax. T>1: flatter distribution → more diversity. In practice: T=0 for factual tasks, T=0.7–1.0 for creative tasks, top-p (nucleus) sampling combined with temperature for best results.
questions.source: Holtzman et al. The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration. ICLR, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.09751

questions.stem: How does ROUGE-L differ from ROUGE-1 as an evaluation metric for text summarisation?

options.A: ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L are identical metrics
options.B: ROUGE-1 measures unigram recall/precision between hypothesis and reference; ROUGE-L measures the longest common subsequence (LCS), capturing sentence-level structural similarity beyond individual word overlap
options.C: ROUGE-L measures semantic similarity; ROUGE-1 measures syntactic similarity
options.D: ROUGE-1 is used for machine translation; ROUGE-L for summarisation

questions.explanation: ROUGE-1: recall = |overlap unigrams| / |reference unigrams|. ROUGE-L: based on LCS length — rewards hypotheses that preserve the order of words in the reference even without exact contiguous matches. ROUGE-L is more sensitive to fluency and coherence than ROUGE-1.
questions.source: Lin. ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries. ACL Workshop, 2004. https://aclanthology.org/W04-1013

questions.stem: Design a prompt to extract structured JSON from this text: 'Dr. Sarah Chen joined MedTech Inc. as CTO on March 15, 2024.' Which prompt best applies structured output extraction principles?

options.A: 'Tell me about Dr. Sarah Chen.'
options.B: 'Extract the person name, organisation, role, and date from the following text and return ONLY valid JSON with keys: name, organisation, role, date. Text: "Dr. Sarah Chen joined MedTech Inc. as CTO on March 15, 2024."'
options.C: 'Summarise the text in one sentence.'
options.D: 'What year did Dr. Chen join MedTech?'

questions.explanation: Structured extraction prompt best practices: (1) specify exact output format (JSON) and key names; (2) instruct 'ONLY valid JSON' to prevent prose wrapping; (3) provide the text explicitly in the prompt; (4) enumerate required fields. Expected output: {"name":"Dr. Sarah Chen","organisation":"MedTech Inc.","role":"CTO","date":"March 15, 2024"}. Options A/C/D are unstructured and produce unpredictable formats.
questions.source: Brown et al. GPT-3. NeurIPS, 2020; Wei et al. CoT. NeurIPS, 2022.

table_of_contents.title: MQ-4 · Prompt Engineering and LLM Evaluation
